1: Elders coming before Ezekiel — vayyavoʾu ʾelai ʾanashim miziqnei Yisraʾel vayyeshvu lifanai — "certain elders of Israel came to me and sat before me" — the sitting-before posture (yashav lifanai) indicates a formal consultation. The elders expect prophetic counsel; what they receive instead is a divine diagnosis of their interior idolatry.
2: Standard word-event formula marks the divine speech that interrupts or reframes the consultation. The elders may not know that YHWH will speak about them rather than to their agenda.
3: ben-ʾadam haʾanashim haʾelle heʿelu gilluleihem ʿal-libam umikshol ʿavonam natenu lineqed paneyhem — "son of man, these men have set up their idols in their hearts, and put the stumbling block of their iniquity before their faces" — gillulim (dung-idols, from gillul, related to gal, dung-heap) is Ezekiel's strongly pejorative term for idols; he uses it ~40 times. The expression gillulim ʿal-libam (idols on/in the heart) distinguishes interior from exterior idolatry: the problem is not necessarily that they have physical idols but that they have given the idol-allegiance of the heart.
4: The principle of the answering-oracle — kol-ish ish mibbet Yisraʾel ʾasher yaʿale ʾet-gillulav ʾal-libam... vavoʾ ʾel-hannavi ʾani YHWH neʿneiti lo vo baʾasher bo bemargoa gillulav — YHWH's declared policy: anyone who comes to a prophet while harboring idols in the heart will receive an answer that meets them in their idolatry ("through the very thing in which he has gone astray"). The ironic judgment mirrors the Pharaoh-hardening dynamic: the seeker's own idolatry becomes the medium of their self-condemnation.
5: The purpose of the answering-oracle — lemaʿan tafos ʾet-beit Yisraʾel belibam — "to lay hold of the house of Israel in their hearts" — tafas (seize/lay hold of) describes divine judicial arrest. The verb used for seizing the idolaters through their idolatry is a form of the lex talionis applied to interior religion: the idol in the heart becomes the means of divine apprehension.
6: The call to repentance — shuvu vehashivu meʿal gilulleikhem — "repent and turn away from your idols" — the double repentance verb: shuvu (turn/return, imperative qal) and hashivu (turn away, hiphil imperative — cause to turn). The two-part construction intensifies the urgency: simply repent + specifically turn away from idols. The hiphil suggests an active act of will distinct from the passive turning.
7: The general principle restated — kol-ish ish mibbet Yisraʾel umin-hagger asher yagur beYisraʾel — now expanded to include "the stranger who sojourns in Israel." The extension to the ger (resident alien) universalizes the principle beyond ethnic boundaries. The Deuteronomic legislation consistently included the ger in covenant obligations (Deut 5:14; 14:21; etc.).
8: The cutting-off (hikkratti) of the idolater from the people — venatattihu leʾot ulimshalim vekhirrattihu miqirb ʿammi — uses two distinct exclusion terms: ʾot (sign, he becomes a living warning-sign) and mashal (proverb/byword, his fate becomes a saying). To be made into a mashal is to have one's life turned into cautionary instruction for others (cf. 5:15; Deut 28:37).
9: The hardest verse in the oracle — vehanavi ki yefutteh vedibbar davar ʾani YHWH pittahti ʾet-hannavi hahu — "if the prophet is deceived and speaks a word, I YHWH have deceived that prophet" — the verb pittah (entice/deceive, piel) applied to YHWH is theologically loaded. The parallel in 1 Kings 22:19-22 (the lying spirit sent to deceive Ahab's prophets) clarifies the mechanism: YHWH permits or uses the prophet's own inclination to self-deception to produce prophetic misguidance. The grammar (ʾani YHWH pittahti, emphatic first-person) makes YHWH's secondary causality explicit without removing the prophet's primary responsibility.
10: Both prophet and inquirer bear their guilt equally — venasaʾu ʿavonam kikhʿavon hadoreshe ken ʿavon hannavi yihyeh — "they shall bear their punishment; the punishment of the inquirer and the punishment of the prophet shall be alike" — the nasa ʿavon formula (bear/carry iniquity) is the standard covenant-curse language for consequence-bearing. The equal distribution of guilt between the deceived inquirer and the deceiving prophet is consistent with the principle of individual accountability developed in ch 18.
11: The ultimate purpose of the prophetic judgment mechanism — lemaʿan loʾ-yitʿu ʿod beit Yisraʾel meʾaḥaray — "so that the house of Israel may no longer go astray from following me." The lemaʿan (in order that) clause grounds even the hardest aspect of ch 14 (YHWH enticing false prophets) in the larger purpose of restoring covenant faithfulness. The goal is restoration of the lev echad (one heart) promised in 11:19.
12: New oracle unit — the four-judgment schema begins. The formal opening — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor followed by a direct address — introduces the distinctive Noah-Daniel-Job speculation.
13: The conditional schema — eres ʾasher teḥeta li limʿol maʿal — "when a land sins against me by acting faithlessly" — maʿal (faithless/treacherous act, specifically covenant violation) is one of Ezekiel's favored terms for Israel's sin. The conditional pattern (four times in vv13, 17, 19, 21) and the response (YHWH sends a judgment agent) creates a systematic catalog of divine judgment tools.
14: vehayu shloshet haʾanashim haʾelle betokha Noaḥ Daniʾel veʾiyyov — the three righteous exemplars: Noah, Daniʾel (spelled differently from the prophet Daniel of the later book — probably the legendary hero of ANE tradition, as the spelling with yod is distinct), and Job. All three are men who were uniquely righteous in contexts of pervasive wickedness, and all were the only survivors in their contexts. The point: even the maximum of righteous intercession cannot avert the judgment once a land has crossed the threshold of covenant violation.
15: The second judgment scenario — lo ḥayya raʿah ʾaʿavir baʾarets — wild beasts as covenant curse (Lev 26:22; Deut 32:24). The desolation of the land (shemamah) is the result, a term that echoes through the book. The ʾaʿavir (hiphil imperfect of ʿavar, cause to pass through) frames the beasts as sent, not merely present.
16: The hypothetical righteous — hamma shloshet haʾanashim baʾtokha ḥay ʾani — YHWH swears by his life (ḥay ʾani, as I live, the divine oath formula) that even Noah, Daniel, and Job could save only themselves. The ḥay ʾani oath formula ("as I live") intensifies the declaration to maximum certainty; it is used when YHWH makes irrevocable pronouncements (also 5:11; 16:48; 17:16, 19, etc.).
17: Third scenario — the sword (ḥerev) as the third covenant-curse instrument. The paired judgments — sword/famine (vv17-18), wild beasts (v15-16), plague (v19-20) — are the "four sore acts of judgment" announced in v21 (shevu mishpatai raʿim).
18: The unchanging conclusion for each scenario — ʾim-benim veʾim-banot yatsillu hemmah levaddam yinnatselut — "they would deliver neither sons nor daughters, but they alone would be saved" — the repeated refrain in each judgment scenario (vv16, 18, 20) establishes the principle of individualized accountability that is fully developed in ch 18. Righteousness is non-transferable; it cannot serve as credit for another person's standing.
19: The fourth scenario — pestilence (dever) and blood (dam) — is the final covenant-curse instrument. The phrase leshafokh dami ʿaleha (to pour out my blood/fury on it) uses dam (blood) in the sense of bloody judgment (the poured-out blood = violent death). The quartet is now complete: famine, wild beasts, sword, plague — the four instruments of Lev 26:14-39.
20: The final application of the three-righteous formula — veNoaḥ Daniʾel veʾiyyov betokha ḥay ʾani... ʾim-ben veʾim-bat yatsillu hemmah levaddam yinnatselut — the ḥay ʾani oath and the hemmah levaddam (they alone) refrain are repeated for the third time. The repetition is not redundancy but legal reinforcement: the principle of non-transferable righteousness is established by the triple oath-formula.
21: ki koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH ʾaf ki ʾarbaat mishpatai haraʿim ḥerev veraʿav veḥayya raʿah vedever shilaḥti ʾel-Yerushalaim — "for thus says Lord YHWH: how much more when I send my four disastrous judgments — sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague — against Jerusalem" — the ʾaf ki (how much more) is the qal-waḥomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if even three hypothetical righteous cannot save anyone in a land under judgment, how much less can Jerusalem claim protection? The four judgments sent simultaneously represent maximum covenant curse.
22: The unexpected conclusion — a remnant survives: vehinneh noteret bo peletah mossiʾim banim uvanot — "behold, there will be survivors in it who will be brought out, sons and daughters" — the survival of survivors (and specifically sons and daughters, the very ones the three righteous of vv14-20 could not save) overturns the schema. The survivors are brought out not by the righteousness of Noah/Daniel/Job but by divine decision. Their purpose: when the watching community sees their behavior (darkeikhem veʾalileyhem, ways and deeds), they will be consoled.
23: The consolation paradox — veniqhamu etchem ki tirʾu ʾet-darkeikhem veʾet-ʾalileyhem viydeʾtem ki loʾ ḥinnami ʾasiti ʾet kol-ʾasher ʾasiti vah — "they will console you when you see their ways and their deeds, and you will know that not without cause have I done what I have done" — the consolation comes not from pleasant news but from the recognition of justice: the behavior of the remnant-survivors demonstrates why the judgment was necessary. The loʾ ḥinnami (not without cause) is YHWH's self-vindication and the community's acceptance of that vindication as their consolation.
